name: TC Mariel + ZED Mariel
scale: 702m berth, 17.5m depth (Neopanamax); ~822k TEU (design up to 3M); zone 465 km², 100% foreign ownership allowed
status: Underused vs capacity; deepwater berth + logistics/warehousing expansion
notes: Terminal operated by PSA (Singapore); zone owned by Almacenes Universales (GAESA — Restricted List).

name: Port of Havana
scale: ~2.2M tons/yr; cruise terminal expanding 2→6 berths; shallow ~11m channel
status: Best positioned for cruise/tourism/ferries + waterfront redevelopment
notes: Cruise terminal under concession to Global Ports Holding (Turkey) + Cuban partner Aries S.A.

name: Port of Santiago de Cuba (Guillermón Moncada)
scale: 231m dock, 3 cranes; upgraded to ~55,000-ton ships; target ~565k tons/yr
status: China-financed modernization; further capacity/connectivity upgrades needed
notes: $120M China Eximbank financing, built by CCCC; Cuban-operated.

claim.title_iii: v. Carnival/Royal Caribbean/Norwegian/MSC + Airbnb; subject to Havana Docks SCOTUS reasoning

name: Port of Cienfuegos
scale: ~11-12m channel; 5 piers + 3 tanker buoys
status: One of Cuba's best natural deep harbors; petrochemical/bulk + refinery revival
notes: Refinery historically Cuvenpetrol (Cuba-Venezuela JV).

name: Port of Moa
scale: ~7-9m pier; serves Moa Nickel (~32,000 t Ni/yr)
status: Sherritt suspended Moa ops in 2026 (fuel crisis) — distressed restructuring; needs deeper berth
notes: Moa Nickel S.A. = Cuban Govt 50% + Sherritt (Canada) 50%.

name: Matanzas Supertanker Base
scale: 2.4M-barrel storage; 4 of 8 tanks destroyed in 2022 fire
status: Slow reconstruction; national energy-resilience rebuild + storage diversification
notes: CUPET; reconstruction backed by Venezuela/China.

name: Port of Antilla / Nipe Bay
type: Sugar export today; deepwater greenfield thesis
scale: Cuba's largest bay; ~14m natural draft; ~80km from Moa nickel belt on Windward Passage
status: Speculative deepwater critical-minerals hub concept; genuine natural-deepwater geography
notes: AntillaPort hub is a marketing concept, not a funded project.

name: National Railway Network (UFC)
scale: ~4,556 km public track (up to ~8,000 km w/ sugar lines); deteriorated
status: Russia (RZD/TMH) modernization plan ~€1.0-1.8B; near-total recapitalization needed
notes: UFC/MITRANS.

name: Central Line (Havana–Santiago)
scale: ~835 km; flagship long-distance service, severely degraded
status: Priority of Russian modernization contract — highest-value rail upgrade target
notes: Havana → Santa Clara → Camagüey → Santiago.

name: José Martí International Airport (HAV)
scale: >4M pax/yr; Terminal 3 expanded Feb 2025; runways/systems deteriorated
status: Govt wants 'most competitive Caribbean hub' — major redevelopment/concession opportunity
notes: ECASA.

name: Jardines del Rey Airport (CCC)
scale: >200,000 pax/yr; runway upgraded 2022
status: Only Cuban airport partly run by a foreign operator (Spain's AENA) — JV template
notes: ECASA + AENA.

name: Autopista Nacional (A1, 'Ocho Vías')
scale: 1970s megaproject never finished; only ~354 km open
status: Eastward completion = flagship greenfield highway opportunity
notes: Havana → Santa Clara → Taguasco (eastern terminus).

name: Antonio Guiteras Thermoelectric Plant
scale: 330 MW; ran at ~25-34% capacity; 9th breakdown of 2026 by May
status: Distressed; 6-month overhaul; highest-impact modernization target
notes: Unión Eléctrica (UNE).

name: Lidio Ramón Pérez 'Felton' Power Plant
scale: 2 × 250 MW; chronic boiler leaks
status: Major repowering candidate
notes: UNE.

name: China-Backed National Solar Program
scale: 92 parks × ~21.87 MW → ~2,000 MW target; ~49 connected (~900-1,000 MW) by Mar 2026
status: Battery storage is the bottleneck — open to financing/EPC/O&M/BESS
notes: UNE + Chinese supply. Solar tripled to >20% of generation in one year.

name: Ciro Redondo Bioelectric Plant
type: Biomass (sugar bagasse) cogeneration — clearest JV success model
scale: 60 MW; replicable across dozens of sugar mills
status: Best foreign-JV precedent in energy
notes: Havana Energy (UK) 51% / Zerus (Cuba) 49%; EPC Shanghai Electric.

name: Boca de Jaruco / Varadero Oil Fields
type: Onshore oil — Enhanced Oil Recovery play
scale: ~40,000 bbl/day vs 90-110k demand; Varadero only ~6-7% extracted
status: EOR is the opportunity
notes: CUPET + Russia's Zarubezhneft JV.

name: La Herradura Wind Farms
scale: ~51 MW design; under construction >10 yrs; turbines erecting Apr 2026
status: Cuba has 21 idle high-potential wind zones
notes: UNE.

name: Albear Aqueduct / Cuenca Vento (Havana supply)
scale: 1861 gravity aqueduct still ~15% of Havana supply; network loses ~58%
status: Needs full main replacement, metering, SCADA for ~2.1M metro
notes: Operated by Aguas de La Habana.

name: Aguas de La Habana (operator) + Almendares wastewater
scale: Serves ~2.1M; untreated wastewater contaminates Vento aquifer
status: Sewerage expansion; 25-yr concession reaching nominal expiry ~2025
notes: Historically INRH 50% + Agbar (Spain) ~45% + Grupo Martinón ~5%.

name: Cayo Coco / Jardines del Rey Desalination
scale: Failing desal in hard-currency tourism zone
status: Clearest path to private/JV (build-operate tied to hotel demand)
notes: Tourism entities.

name: Moa Nickel + Cobalt Belt
scale: Holguín holds ~34% of world Ni reserves; Moa ~32,000 t Ni/yr
status: Sherritt suspended 2026; recapitalization/restructuring opportunity
notes: Strategic critical minerals (Ni, Co).

name: Las Camariocas Nickel Project
scale: Long-stalled Soviet-era nickel plant
status: Revival/greenfield thesis adjacent to Moa belt
notes: Approximate coordinates.

name: Vuelta Abajo Tobacco Region
type: Premium cigar tobacco — world's best terroir
scale: Source of Habanos premium cigars (global luxury brand)
status: Private farms (vegueros) exist; Habanos S.A. distribution is a JV
notes: Habanos S.A. = Cuban state + (historically) Imperial/Allied. Private growers = potential Layer-1.

name: AZCUBA Sugar Mills Network
scale: Dozens of mills; output collapsed to historic lows
status: Cogeneration (biomass) + ethanol modernization across mills
notes: Ciro Redondo biomass JV is the template.

name: Sierra Maestra Coffee Highlands
scale: Historic export crop; production well below potential
status: Smallholder/private revival; specialty-export thesis
notes: Smallholder coops/private farms can be Layer-1 with proper structuring.

name: CIGB / BioCubaFarma Biotech Complex
scale: Cuba's globally-respected biotech sector (vaccines, monoclonals, Heberprot-P)
status: World-class IP; export & licensing potential
notes: State (BioCubaFarma). Heavy sanctions sensitivity.

name: Old Havana (Habana Vieja) Restoration
scale: UNESCO World Heritage core; private paladares & casas particulares thriving
status: Private hospitality (restaurants, B&Bs, boutique) = Layer-1 deal flow
notes: State hotels = atlas; private paladares/casas = investable Layer-1.

name: Trinidad (Heritage Tourism Town)
scale: UNESCO colonial town; dense private B&B / restaurant economy
status: Strong private-sector hospitality deal flow
notes: Layer-1: vetted private hospitality MIPYMEs.

name: Havana MIPYME Cluster
type: Licensed private SMEs (paladares, retail, services, light manufacturing)
scale: Largest concentration of Cuba's 11,000+ legalized MIPYMEs
status: INVESTABLE NOW — diaspora/foreign equity allowed since Mar 18 2026; deploy via QvaPay wallets
notes: Layer-1 core. Each deal: KYC + OFAC SDN screen + ≤100-employee private-entity check + OFAC counsel.

name: Pinar del Río Private Farms
scale: Private vegueros + food producers
status: INVESTABLE NOW — private agricultural MIPYMEs
notes: Layer-1 with structuring.

name: Cuba Emprende Graduate Network
type: Trained private entrepreneurs (Slyk + QvaPay bundle pipeline)
scale: ~5,800+ graduates / ~4,070 businesses (Proyecto Cuba Emprende)
status: INVESTABLE NOW — pre-trained, high-intent MIPYMEs; activation via Slyk store + QvaPay wallet
notes: Layer-1 distribution channel + deal flow.

name: Cuban Electric Company (national grid)
scale: Largest single certified claim
status: Confiscated 1960; #1 certified claim
notes: Tallapiedra & Regla thermo plants + grid.

name: Cuban Telephone Co. (ITT)
scale: $181.8M combined ITT/Starwood-linked
status: Seized Mar 1959; certified claim
notes: Marriott controls the claim.

name: Belot Oil Refinery (Esso/Exxon)
scale: $71.6M certified; Exxon now claims >$1B
status: ACTIVE Title III suit

claim.title_iii: Exxon v. CIMEX & CUPET (D.D.C.) — ongoing, D.C. Cir. remanded 2025

notes: 9th-largest certified claim.

name: Texaco Refinery (Santiago)
scale: $50.1M certified
status: Confiscated 1960
notes: Now Hermanos Díaz refinery site.

name: United Fruit Sugar (Boston & Preston mills)
scale: $85.1M certified
status: Confiscated 1960
notes: 4th-largest certified claim.

name: Coca-Cola Bottling (Havana)
scale: $27.5M certified
status: Confiscated 1960
notes: Plants in Havana, Santiago + 4 cities.

name: Colgate-Palmolive Factory (Havana)
scale: $14.5M certified
status: Confiscated 1960

name: Havana Docks Cruise Terminal
type: Confiscated cruise/cargo piers — landmark Title III case
scale: ~$400M+ judgment (trebled)
status: SCOTUS WIN May 21 2026 (8-1)

claim.title_iii: v. Royal Caribbean/Carnival/Norwegian/MSC — affirmed by SCOTUS 2026

notes: The case that broadened Title III.

name: El Encanto Department Store
scale: Cuba's largest store
status: Nationalized 1959; destroyed by arson 1961
notes: Galiano & San Rafael.

name: Hotel Nacional / Habana Riviera
scale: Iconic Vedado hotels
status: Nationalized 1960; Lansky-linked Riviera heirs sought compensation

name: Bacardí Original Distillery (Ronera Santiago)
type: Confiscated rum distillery — birthplace of Bacardí (1862)
scale: Founding Bacardí site
status: Seized Oct 14 1960; now state Ronera Santiago
notes: World's largest family spirits co.; rebuilt in PR/Mexico.

name: Edificio Bacardí (Bacardi Building)
scale: 1930 Art Deco icon, bronze bat
status: Nationalized; now govt offices + bar
notes: Monserrate & San Juan de Dios, Old Havana.

name: Arechabala Distillery / Havana Club origin
scale: 150-acre complex; created Havana Club
status: Seized Jan 1 1960; rights sold to Bacardi 1994-95

claim.title_iii: Havana Club trademark war: Bacardi (US) vs Pernod/Cubaexport (world)

notes: Centerpiece trademark dispute; courts upheld Cuba's renewal 2025-26.

name: Partagás Cigar Factory (Cifuentes)
scale: 2nd best-selling Cuban cigar 1950s
status: Confiscated 1960; building closed (2020 roof collapse)

claim.title_iii: Rodríguez heirs sued over Partagás building 2020

notes: Calle Industria 520, behind Capitolio.

name: H. Upmann Factory / Montecristo (Menéndez-García)
type: Confiscated cigar factory — Montecristo created here (1935)
scale: World's top-selling cigar brand (Montecristo)
status: Confiscated Sept 15 1960; family fled to DR

claim.current_holder: Habanos S.A. (Cuban) / Altadis USA, La Romana DR (non-Cuban)

notes: Benji Menéndez became master blender at General Cigar.

name: Hoyo de Monterrey / Punch (Palicio)
scale: ~13% of Havana cigar exports 1958
status: Confiscated 1959-60; Palicio left for FL

name: Romeo y Julieta Cigar Factory
scale: World-famous brand
status: Confiscated after 1959
notes: Belascoaín building (shared w/ El Rey del Mundo).

name: Antillana de Acero 'José Martí' Steelworks
scale: ~1,700 workers; new EAF 2023
status: Modernization-ready, partly operating (Russian credit)
notes: GESIME.

name: Cementos Cienfuegos ('Karl Marx')
scale: Fuel-constrained; modernization need
status: Partially operating
notes: Historically w/ Votorantim (Brazil).

name: Cementos Mariel ('René Arcay')
scale: ~1,500 kt; w/ Cienfuegos = >85% national output
status: Fuel-constrained; modernization-ready

name: Las Camariocas Nickel Plant (unfinished)
scale: Construction suspended ~1996
status: IDLE / never completed — greenfield completion
notes: Energy-intensive old tech caveat.

name: Nicaro Nickel Plant ('René Ramos Latour')
scale: Caron-process, obsolete
status: Largely IDLE — brownfield redevelopment
notes: Also a certified claim.

name: Camilo Cienfuegos Oil Refinery
scale: Intermittent on Russian crude
status: Modernization + feedstock opportunity
notes: Originally PDVSA JV.

name: Textilera Ariguanabo (Bauta)
type: Cotton/rayon textile mill — once Cuba's largest non-sugar factory
scale: >2,400 workers in 1958
status: LARGELY IDLE/ABANDONED — major brownfield revival

name: Papelera Pulpa Cuba (Trinidad)
scale: Built 1957-59 (US tech); output ~9.5% of 1989
status: Severely degraded — modernization/revival

name: Central Hershey / Camilo Cienfuegos (sugar)
type: Historic sugar mill + company town + electric railway
scale: Closed ~2002-03 (1,300 jobs)
status: Abandoned industrial ruin — heritage/tourism + redevelopment
notes: Hershey electric rail line still notable.

name: Cervecería Bucanero (Holguín)
scale: ~1M hl/yr; targeting 2M
status: Operating, expansion-stage
notes: Historically Cuba + AB InBev/Labatt.

name: ZED Mariel (Special Development Zone)
scale: 62+ approved businesses; 35-project portfolio
status: Active — food, biotech, chemicals, logistics, real estate
notes: Foreign concessionaires (BrasCuba, Richmeat, Profood). 100% foreign ownership permitted in-zone — still U.S.-restricted.

name: Juan Gualberto Gómez Airport (VRA)
scale: Cuba's 2nd-busiest airport; ~1.3M+ pax/yr (peak); single 3,500 m runway; 1 international terminal
status: Operational; serves the Varadero resort corridor. Terminal repaired/upgraded 2024 under the 2024-2030 airport modernization plan. Concession/JV opportunity in terminal operations and ground services.
notes: Operated by ECASA (state airport operator). No current foreign operator.

name: Frank País Airport (HOG)
scale: Single 3,238 m runway with ILS; terminal ~1,200 pax/hr after 2007 expansion
status: Operational; gateway to Guardalavaca beaches. Comfort/AC upgrades ongoing under the 2024-2030 plan. Open to foreign concession/modernization.
notes: Operated by ECASA (state). No current foreign operator.

name: Antonio Maceo Airport (SCU)
scale: Main runway ~4,002 m plus secondary 1,400 m; 1 terminal
status: Operational; principal airport of eastern Cuba. Full renovation under evaluation for 2025. Concession/upgrade opportunity.
notes: Operated by ECASA (state). No current foreign operator.

name: Abel Santamaría Airport (SNU)
scale: Single ~3,017 m runway; 1 terminal; ~10 km from Santa Clara
status: Operational; main gateway for Cayo Santa María resorts. In national runway-safety and modernization upgrades. Open to terminal/services investment.
notes: Operated by ECASA (state). No current foreign operator.

name: Ignacio Agramonte International Airport (CMW)
scale: Single 3,000 m runway; 1 terminal
status: Operational; serves Camagüey city and Santa Lucía beach. AC/comfort improvements ongoing under the 2024-2030 plan. Concession/modernization opportunity.
notes: Operated by ECASA (state). No current foreign operator.

name: Jaime González Airport (CFG)
scale: Single 2,400 m runway; ~4 km from Cienfuegos
status: Operational with limited scheduled international traffic. In the national runway-safety program. Opportunity to grow tourism/charter capacity.
notes: Operated by ECASA (state). No current foreign operator.

name: Vilo Acuña Airport (CYO)
province: Isla de la Juventud (Cayo Largo del Sur)
scale: Single 3,000 m runway; 1 terminal
status: Operational; dedicated resort gateway for Cayo Largo del Sur. Rehabilitation completed under the 2024-2030 plan. Open to concession tied to resort development.
notes: Operated by ECASA (state). No current foreign operator.

name: Sierra Maestra Airport (MZO)
scale: Single 3,000 m runway; ~12 km from Manzanillo
status: Operational; serves Manzanillo and Granma with mainly domestic and seasonal charter flights. Upside tied to southeastern tourism growth.
notes: Operated by ECASA (state). No current foreign operator.

name: Hermanos Ameijeiras Airport (VTU)
scale: Single ~1,819 m runway; small terminal
status: Small regional airport serving Las Tunas, primarily domestic Cubana service. Needs runway/terminal investment for international or larger aircraft.
notes: Operated by ECASA (state). No current foreign operator.

name: Gustavo Rizo Airport (BCA)
scale: Single 1,850 m runway; daytime-only; small terminal
status: Small domestic airport serving Baracoa, daytime-only. Niche eco/heritage-tourism upside; needs lighting/runway investment.
notes: Operated by ECASA (state). No current foreign operator.

name: Port of Nuevitas (Puerto Tarafa / Pastelillo)
type: Bulk cargo / sugar / fertilizer / cement port
scale: Berths across Pastelillo, Puerto Tarafa, Bufadero; max draft ~10.3 m; tankers up to ~14,000 DWT; ~40 calls/yr
status: Operational but aging — a principal sugar-export and bulk-import port (fertilizer, grain, cement). Narrow channel limits vessel size. Opportunity in dredging and terminal modernization.
notes: Camagüey's main commercial port; state port authority. Adjacent to the Diez de Octubre thermo plant.

name: Port of Cárdenas
scale: Single pier ~750 m, 4 berths; shallow bay limits draft
status: Operational regional port on Cárdenas Bay — sugar exports plus agricultural/industrial cargo. Near the Varadero tourism corridor. Opportunity in cargo-handling upgrades and dredging.
notes: UN/LOCODE CUCAR. State-operated. Distinct from the Matanzas Supertanker Base on the adjacent bay.

name: Port of Manzanillo
scale: Cargo worked at anchor; anchorage LOA up to 200 m, draft ~10 m; petroleum via conventional buoy mooring
status: Operational but constrained — transshipment for sugar, tobacco, timber; CBM handles petroleum imports. Reef-limited, daylight-only navigation. Opportunity in fixed-berth construction and channel/aids improvement.
notes: On the Gulf of Guacanayabo near the Cauto delta; state-operated. Also a regional fishing base. UN/LOCODE CUMZO.

name: Port of Casilda (Trinidad)
scale: One pier, 2 berths; small vessels; loading also at anchor in Casilda Bay
status: Small operational port serving Trinidad (5 km inland, UNESCO city). Historic sugar/rum export point; now minor cargo, fishing and small-vessel/yacht calls. Strong tourism-linked upside near Playa Ancón.
notes: Casilda Bay enclosed by the Ancón Peninsula; state-operated. Rail terminus to Trinidad. UN/LOCODE CUCAS.

name: Port of Isabela de Sagua
scale: Shallow-draft north-coast port; small-vessel berths plus marina; rail link to Sagua La Grande sugar valley
status: Diminished — once a busy sugar/tobacco export port; now low-volume fishing and a small marina, threatened by sea-level rise. Opportunity is largely tourism/marina rather than heavy cargo.
notes: Northern terminus of the rail line serving Sagua La Grande mills; state-operated.

name: Puerto Carúpano (Cayo Juan Claro, Puerto Padre Bay)
scale: Bulk sugar terminal on Cayo Juan Claro (causeway-linked); deep, protected Puerto Padre Bay; bulk sugar/molasses loading, fuel-oil discharge
status: North-coast bulk-sugar export terminal (1978), serving the Antonio Guiteras mill and Camagüey/Holguín output. Operational but underutilized amid the sugar-industry collapse. Opportunity in terminal rehab tied to any sugar/agro revival.
notes: On Cayo Juan Claro in Puerto Padre Bay; state-operated. Counterpart to Guayabal on the south coast. Coordinates approximate.

name: Port of Guayabal
scale: Single pier ~156 m; bulk sugar loaded by onshore grab cranes / ship's gear; south-coast Gulf of Guacanayabo
status: Largely abandoned/derelict as of 2026 — local reporting (Apr 2026) calls it a 'cemetery of iron and rust,' non-operational despite deep-water access and proximity to the U.S. East Coast. High-upside rehabilitation/greenfield bulk-terminal opportunity.
notes: Operated by Empresa Terminales Mambisas de La Habana (state). Cuba's first bulk-sugar terminal site. UN/LOCODE CUGYB. Coordinates approximate.

name: CTE Máximo Gómez (Mariel)
scale: ~400 MW nameplate (Soviet-era ~90 MW units; effective output far lower)
status: Severely degraded 2025-2026. A 2022 fire knocked out Units 6-7 (~170 MW); Unit 6 returned then went offline again Sept 2025 on oil contamination and pump faults. Chronic outages. Opportunity: full retrofit/repowering.
notes: Operated by Unión Eléctrica (UNE) under MINEM via the SEN national grid.

name: CTE Ernesto Guevara (Santa Cruz del Norte)
scale: ~295-300 MW (two ~100 MW + one ~95 MW; Soviet technology)
status: One of the larger western plants, plagued by frequent unit trips through 2025-2026. Burns national crude via pipeline (lower fuel-transport cost). Opportunity: unit overhaul/repowering.
notes: Also called 'Este de la Habana'. Operated by Unión Eléctrica (UNE) under MINEM.

name: CTE Carlos Manuel de Céspedes (Cienfuegos)
scale: 316 MW (two ~158 MW oil-fired units)
status: Repeatedly down through 2025: Unit 3 tripped on a boiler leak then a condenser fault; Unit 4 under maintenance. Long repair cycles add to the national deficit. Opportunity: boiler/condenser rehabilitation.
notes: Operated by Unión Eléctrica (UNE) under MINEM.

name: CTE Antonio Maceo (Renté)
scale: ~380 MW nameplate (four ~95 MW Soviet-era units); real output ~258 MW
status: Critically aged (in service since the late 1960s). Frequent furnace leaks and vacuum/feed-water faults caused eastern-Cuba blackouts through 2025-2026. Opportunity: comprehensive rehabilitation or replacement.
notes: On the Renté peninsula, Santiago Bay. Operated by Unión Eléctrica (UNE) under MINEM.

name: CTE Diez de Octubre (Nuevitas)
scale: ~280-375 MW (~125 MW units; sources vary)
status: Operating but unreliable — a boiler caught fire Dec 2025; multiple unit trips through 2025-2026. Major contributor to the central-Cuba deficit. Opportunity: boiler replacement/modernization.
notes: At Punta de Domingo, Nuevitas Bay. Operated by Unión Eléctrica (UNE) under MINEM.

name: CTE Otto Parellada (Tallapiedra)
scale: ~60-64 MW (single legacy unit)
status: Small, very old urban plant on Havana Bay; a 64 MW unit (1972) ran until ~2024, intermittently feeding ~45 MW. Site sits on the historic Havana grid tied to the confiscated Cuban Electric Company — see /claims. Opportunity: limited; value mainly as urban land/peaker.
notes: Operated by Unión Eléctrica (UNE) under MINEM. Overlaps the pre-1959 Havana grid in the Cuban Electric Company confiscation claim — not treated as a separate claim here. Coordinates approximate.

name: Energas Boca de Jaruco (Sherritt JV)
scale: ~150 MW combined-cycle (part of Energas' ~480-506 MW across Boca de Jaruco, Varadero & Puerto Escondido)
status: Among Cuba's cheapest, cleanest power — burns associated gas from oil production. Energas supplies ~8-10% of national generation but faces gas-supply/maintenance constraints. Opportunity: gas-capture expansion, though the JV slot is held by Sherritt.
notes: Energas S.A. JV: Sherritt (Canada) ~1/3; Unión Eléctrica (UNE) + CUPET ~2/3. Under MINEM.

name: Karpowership Floating Powerships (Cuba fleet)
scale: Up to ~420 MW across the fleet at peak (e.g. Suheyla Sultan ~240 MW; contract ~300 MW)
status: Highly volatile — at peak (2023-24) up to 8 Turkish barges off Havana, Mariel and Santiago supplied ~a quarter of Cuba's power. Most departed by May 2025 over unpaid fuel/operating costs; one returned to Havana April 2026. Opportunity is large but financing is the bottleneck.
notes: Operated by Karpowership (Karadeniz Holding, Türkiye) under contract to UNE/MINEM; vessels are leased and mobile, not Cuban-owned. Coordinates approximate (Havana Bay anchorage).

name: Hershey Electric Railway (Casablanca–Hershey–Matanzas)
scale: ~92 km, standard gauge, 600 V DC (Cuba's only electric railway); rolling stock = ex-FGC cars ~60 yrs old
status: Severely degraded; mostly suspended since Hurricane Irma (2017), partial Hershey–Jaruco service since 2018. A June 2025 'revival' plan would switch to diesel and restore Matanzas station as heritage. Heritage/tourism rehabilitation opportunity.
notes: Operated by Unión de Ferrocarriles de Cuba (UFC) under MITRANS. Built in the 1920s by the Hershey Chocolate Corp.

name: Estación Central de La Habana (Havana Central Station)
scale: Principal national terminal (1912); restoration since 2017; solar panels being added to platforms
status: Under long-running restoration (delayed by crisis, sanctions, pandemic); partial reopening targeted late 2025/early 2026 per MITRANS. Second phase covers offices and a commercial gallery — adaptive-reuse/commercial concession opportunity.
notes: Owned/operated by Unión de Ferrocarriles de Cuba (UFC) under MITRANS. National Monument; hub of the national and Havana suburban networks.

name: National Rolling Stock & Locomotive Recapitalization
scale: RZD framework ~€1.888B (~$2B); RA-2 EMUs, LDE-2500 locos, TGM shunters (Russia); ~80 of ~250 Chinese carriages delivered
status: Chronically under-delivered on Cuban payment incapacity; formally resumed Sept 2024 under a March 2024 Russia-Cuba credit agreement. Major equipment-supply and financing opportunity (state-to-state, non-U.S.).
notes: Procured by Unión de Ferrocarriles de Cuba (UFC) under MITRANS. Suppliers: TMH/Sinara (Russia) via RZD; CRRC-type stock (China) — sanctioned counterparties.

name: Sugar-Mill Railway Network (AZCUBA)
scale: ~7,742 km of plantation track (2003): ~65% standard, ~35% narrow gauge; current operable mileage far lower
status: Heavily contracted with the collapse of sugar output (mass mill closures since 2002); much track abandoned or steam-era. Niche heritage/tourism (steam) and freight-rehab potential where mills remain active.
notes: Operated by the AZCUBA sugar group (formerly MINAZ), separate from the public UFC network.

name: Havana Suburban Railway
scale: 8 lines from 3 terminals (Central, Tulipán, Casablanca); standard gauge; only the Hershey line electrified
status: Partially operational/degraded — several lines non-operational, some seasonal. Commuter-rail rehabilitation and EMU re-equipment opportunity serving Havana, Artemisa, Mayabeque, Matanzas.
notes: Operated by Unión de Ferrocarriles de Cuba (UFC) under MITRANS; the Casablanca line is the Hershey electric line.

name: Carretera Central (CN)
scale: ~1,139 km original (1927-31); ~1,435 km modern, mostly 2-lane
status: Aging 1920s spine from La Fe (Pinar del Río) to Baracoa (Guantánamo), linking nearly all provincial capitals. Worn pavement, narrow lanes, no shoulders — large-scale rehabilitation/capacity-upgrade opportunity.
notes: Controlled by MITRANS. Original 1927-31 build under President Machado; U.S. contractor Warren Bros. (Boston) involved. Representative point near the central spine.

name: Vía Blanca (Havana–Matanzas–Varadero)
scale: ~138 km (85 km Havana–Matanzas + ~53 km to Varadero); mostly 4-lane
status: One of Cuba's busiest highways, the tourism-critical Havana–Varadero link. The Matanzas–Varadero segment is Cuba's only inter-city toll road — resurfacing/widening and toll-concession (PPP) potential.
notes: MITRANS. Runs along the Straits of Florida shoreline; part of the Circuito Norte.

name: Puente de Bacunayagua
scale: ~314 m long, ~103-110 m high; 41 slender concrete columns
status: Cuba's tallest and longest bridge, an iconic Vía Blanca landmark over the Bacunayagua canyon (Mayabeque/Matanzas border). Built 1957-59. Major tourist viewpoint; long-term structural maintenance and tourism-infrastructure upside.
notes: MITRANS. Inaugurated 1959; marks the Mayabeque–Matanzas boundary ~86 km east of Havana.

name: Túnel de la Bahía de La Habana (Havana Bay Tunnel)
scale: ~733 m long, ~12 m below ground; immersed-tube under the bay
status: Operating since 1958; the sole road link under Havana harbor connecting the city to East Havana and the Vía Blanca/Matanzas corridor. Critical 1950s chokepoint with rehabilitation/safety-systems and capacity needs.
notes: MITRANS. Built 1957-58 by France's Société de Grands Travaux de Marseille; opened May 1958.

name: Circuito Norte (Northern Circuit)
scale: ~1,222 km, predominantly 2-lane; incorporates the 138 km Vía Blanca
status: Second-longest Cuban highway, west-east along the north coast linking Cárdenas, Sagua, Remedios, Caibarién, Morón and Holguín — the access spine for north-coast cayo tourism. Variable condition; rehabilitation/tourism-access upgrade opportunity.
notes: MITRANS. Feeds the Caibarién and Morón causeways to the northern cays.

name: Pedraplén Caibarién–Cayo Santa María
scale: ~48 km stone causeway with ~46 bridge openings
status: One of the world's longest sea causeways (built 1989-1999); the sole vehicular access to the Cayo Santa María resort enclave. Tourism-revenue-linked asset — maintenance, bridge-opening and resort-access expansion potential.
notes: Roadway under MITRANS; toll-gated access from Caibarién tied to MINTUR tourism entities.

name: Pedraplén Cayo Coco (Morón–Cayo Coco Causeway)
scale: ~27 km stone causeway across Bahía de Perros; ~3 million m³ of stone
status: Built 1988 across the 'Bay of Dogs' from Morón to Cayo Coco — primary access to Cayo Coco/Cayo Guillermo resorts. Documented water-flow/environmental concerns; upgrade and additional bridge-opening opportunity.
notes: Roadway under MITRANS; toll-gated from Morón; resort access tied to MINTUR entities.
